assertions.text: Table uses value-set syntax (curly braces like {READ, WRITE} or {READ, WRITE, DELETE}) to express rules that hold across multiple actions — rather than one row per role/action combination
assertions.evidence:
Admin can do anything       | ADMIN | {READ, WRITE, DELETE} | true
    User can read and write     | USER  | {READ, WRITE}         | true
    Guest cannot write or delete| GUEST | {WRITE, DELETE}       | false

assertions.text: Table has fewer than 9 data rows (i.e. does not enumerate every role/action combination individually)
assertions.evidence: The table contains exactly 5 data rows: 'Admin can do anything', 'User can read and write', 'User cannot delete', 'Guest can read', 'Guest cannot write or delete'

assertions.text: Test method body contains no if or switch statements
Table has a scenario/description column as the leftmost column
No two rows share both the same Role value and the same boolean output — forces value set consolidation (e.g. USER can READ and USER can WRITE, both true, should be one row with {READ, WRITE}).
assertions.evidence: USER appears in two rows but with different outputs: 'User can read and write | USER | {READ, WRITE} | true' and 'User cannot delete | USER | DELETE | false'. GUEST similarly: 'Guest can read | GUEST | READ | true' and 'Guest cannot write or delete| GUEST | {WRITE, DELETE} | false'. No two rows share both same Role and same boolean output.

assertions.text: Test method has either a @DisplayName annotation or a method name that reads as a clear, descriptive title when converted from camelCase/snake_case (e.g. 'permissionsByRoleAndAction' → 'Permissions By Role And Action'). Not a generic name like 'test1' or 'canPerform'.
assertions.evidence: void shouldEnforcePermissions(Role role, Action action, boolean canPerform)

assertions.text: @Description is present and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as where permissions are checked in the request lifecycle, or open questions. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
assertions.evidence: @Description("ADMIN has full access; USER can read and write but not delete; GUEST is read-only") — this merely restates/summarises what the table rows already show (ADMIN has {READ,WRITE,DELETE}=true, USER has {READ,WRITE}=true and DELETE=false, GUEST has READ=true and {WRITE,DELETE}=false). It provides no context about where permissions are checked in the lifecycle, no open questions, and no information beyond the rows.

assertions.text: Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
assertions.evidence:
@Description("ADMIN has full access; USER can read and write but not delete; GUEST is read-only")
@TableTest(""" — @Description appears before @TableTest, with no @DisplayName present, matching the required order.

assertions.text: @Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
assertions.evidence: @Description("ADMIN has full access; USER can read and write but not delete; GUEST is read-only") — the text is a single short line and not long, so a regular string (not a text block) is acceptable. No string concatenation with + is used.
